title: Lady Bouviers Lover

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Opening credits sequence with Homer arriving home
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature exclamation of frustration
jokes.explanation: Homer's catchphrase - perfectly integrated character moment, maximum economy, highly quotable but not original at this point in the series

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Please, Krusty, this is very demeaning. / Shut up and conduct!
jokes.setup: Sideshow Bob objecting to whatever humiliating task he's doing
jokes.punchline: Krusty's dismissive command to continue
jokes.explanation: Good character dynamic - Bob's sophistication vs Krusty's crudeness. Well-structured but fairly standard for their relationship

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: But Mr. Krusty, I must object! This is beneath my dignity as a performer!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Ah, quit yer bellyachin' and get back out there!

jokes.text: What? It's not 'Magaggie's' birthday?
jokes.setup: Homer mispronouncing Maggie's name while frosting her cake poorly
jokes.punchline: His confusion about whose birthday it is
jokes.explanation: Homer's typical obliviousness combined with mispronunciation. Functional character comedy but nothing exceptional

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Happy birthday, Maggie! Or is it Marge's birthday? Wait, whose birthday is it again?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, it's Maggie's birthday. You've been frosting her cake for the last hour.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, right. Happy birthday, Maggie! Or Maggie? Magina? Whatever.

jokes.text: Hey! Hey! Hey! Stop it! I made a special cake for you to ruin.
jokes.setup: Homer ruining Maggie's birthday cake
jokes.punchline: Marge revealing she made a separate cake specifically for Homer to destroy
jokes.explanation: Excellent character writing - Marge's practical solution to Homer's destructiveness. Great economy and structure with unexpected reveal

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you destroyed Maggie's birthday cake!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I made a separate cake specifically for you to destroy.

jokes.text: Hello, everyone, except Homer.
jokes.setup: Grampa arriving for Maggie's birthday party
jokes.punchline: Specifically excluding Homer from his greeting
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character consistency - Grampa's hostility toward Homer in minimal words

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, hello everyone! Happy birthday, Maggie! Marge, Bart, Lisa, Maggie... and... oh, Homer's here too.

jokes.text: Marge, I think that's your father-in-law across the street.
jokes.setup: Someone pointing out Grampa across the street
jokes.punchline: The visual of him doing something embarrassing in public
jokes.explanation: Standard visual gag setup - embarrassing relative spotted in public. Functional but predictable

jokes.text: We didn't invite other babies. Maggie doesn't seem to get along with the other babies.
jokes.setup: Question about waiting for other babies at the party
jokes.punchline: Treating infant social dynamics like adult personality conflicts
jokes.explanation: Absurd premise treating babies like adults with social issues. Well-earned through Marge's matter-of-fact delivery

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, we're waiting for the other babies to arrive.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: You know how they can be – so cliquish.

jokes.text: Put some Lister's Carbolic Unguent on a wad of cotton... That'll stop them shakes.
jokes.setup: Old-fashioned medical advice for baby Maggie
jokes.punchline: Archaic remedy with medical-sounding nonsense
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Grampa's outdated medical knowledge. Authentic period references make it earned

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Back in my day, we didn't need fancy doctors for babies. A little leeches on the gums, some mercury tonic, and a good bloodletting would clear right up whatever ailed 'em. Worked every time!

jokes.text: No, no. What she needs is a balsam specific... While we're burning money, why don't we give her a curative galvanic belt too?
jokes.setup: Competing old-fashioned medical advice
jokes.punchline: Grampa's sarcastic escalation with even more absurd treatments
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the medical advice bit. 'Galvanic belt' is a nice specific period detail

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: You should apply leeches to draw out the bad humors.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Leeches? That's nothing. I'm gonna saw off my leg, pour hot tar on the stump, and sneeze into a jar of mayonnaise.

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: That's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: You're right. I should also drink my own bathwater and have a barber drill a hole in my skull to let the sickness out.

jokes.text: Don't forget to give her Smeckler's Powder. / Don't make fun.
jokes.setup: Continuing the old medicine routine
jokes.punchline: Made-up product name 'Smeckler's Powder' and Jacqueline's earnest defense
jokes.explanation: Continues the medical advice bit with silly product name. Functional but starting to wear thin

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Back in my day, we used Smeckler's Powder for everything.

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: Smeckler's Powder? I've never heard of it.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Of course you haven't. They discontinued it. Probably because of the asbestos.

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: Asbestos? That's terrible!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Terrible? It cured everything! Headaches, heartburn, open sores...

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: That doesn't sound safe at all.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Safe? Lady, we didn't have the word 'safe' back then. We just had 'dead' and 'not dead yet.'

jokes.text: Oh! That was a real horn-honker!
jokes.setup: Remembering Lisa's third birthday performance
jokes.punchline: Grampa's old-fashioned expression of approval
jokes.explanation: Authentic old-timey slang that fits Grampa perfectly. Simple but character-appropriate

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: That's a real humdinger!

jokes.text: Hot dogs, Armour hot dogs... What kinds of kids eat Armour hot dogs... Fat kids, skinny kids, kids who climb on rocks...
jokes.setup: Being forced to perform their childhood song
jokes.punchline: The song is a commercial jingle for hot dogs
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of commercialized childhood - the 'adorable' song is actually corporate jingle. Great cultural commentary

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh no, not the hot dog song.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh yes. 'Hot dogs, armour hot dogs...'

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, we're not doing this.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Come on, sing it with me! 'Frankly, they'd have us all day if we let 'em!'

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I can't believe we used to perform this commercial jingle for our parents.

jokes.text: Doesn't this family know any songs that aren't commercials?
jokes.setup: After hearing the Armour hot dogs jingle
jokes.punchline: Pointing out the family only knows advertising jingles
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic observation with maximum economy. Brilliant social commentary about commercialization of culture

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's it! We don't know any songs anymore, just advertising jingles!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's not true, Homer. We know lots of songs.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Name one!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: The Meow Mix song!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Plop plop, fizz fizz...

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: *sucks pacifier to the tune of the Simpsons theme*

jokes.text: I feel like chicken tonight, like chicken tonight, like chicken tonight
jokes.setup: Jacqueline's question about non-commercial songs
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately proving her point with another jingle
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback structure - Homer unwittingly proves Jacqueline's point immediately. Excellent timing and character work

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline
dialogue.line: Why does everything have to be a jingle? Can't you just enjoy music for music's sake, without it being about selling something?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You're right, you're right. Here, I'll sing you a song with no commercial purpose whatsoever. *clears throat* 'See my vest, see my vest, made of real gorilla chest...'

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline
dialogue.line: Homer, that's a jingle for your vest made of gorilla fur.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, how about this one? 'Duff beer for me, Duff beer for you, I'll have a Duff, you'll have one too!'

jokes.text: He's on in five minutes! / Step on it! / Each Matlock could be our last.
jokes.setup: Grampa and Jacqueline rushing to watch Matlock
jokes.punchline: The implication that they might die before the next episode
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about elderly mortality wrapped in urgency about TV shows. Good character moment with morbid twist

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: We better hurry or we'll miss Matlock.

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline
dialogue.line: We might not live to see the next one.

jokes.text: That is funny.
jokes.setup: Marge saying both parents seem lonely
jokes.punchline: Homer's literal, obtuse response missing her point entirely
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obliviousness - missing emotional subtext for literal interpretation. Good character consistency

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Both of your parents seem so lonely.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So? We're not lonely. We got each other.

jokes.text: Old people don't need companionship. They need to be isolated and studied so it can be determined what nutrients they have that might be extracted for our personal use.
jokes.setup: Marge suggesting elderly companionship
jokes.punchline: Homer's dystopian view of elderly as research subjects for nutrient extraction
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark and original concept - treating elderly like lab animals. Unexpected escalation with specific details about nutrient extraction

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, why don't you spend time with some elderly people? It would be good for you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Elderly people? Marge, they're just walking research experiments. We should be extracting their nutrients before they expire. It's called efficiency.

jokes.text: Homer, would you please stop reading that Ross Perot pamphlet?
jokes.setup: Homer's disturbing view of the elderly
jokes.punchline: Revealing his dystopian ideas come from a political pamphlet
jokes.explanation: Great topical reference explaining Homer's weird ideas. Good structure revealing the source of his dystopian thinking

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, we should just put all the old people on an ice floe and let them drift away.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer! That's terrible!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I read it in this pamphlet.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It had some really good ideas.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Let me see that.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, this is from the 'Kill All Oldsters Party'!

jokes.text: That's not my mother. / I'll be back in a jiffy!
jokes.setup: Homer going to pick up someone he thinks is Marge's mother
jokes.punchline: He's got the wrong person but continues anyway
jokes.explanation: Homer's oblivious confidence even when wrong. Good visual comedy setup with his characteristic persistence

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hello, Marge's mother! Let me help you with your bags.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown Woman
dialogue.line: I think you have me confused with someone else.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, no, I'd recognize that... unique smell anywhere. Come on, let's go home.

jokes.text: Oh, it's not a swindle. What you do is, see, you give them all your credit card numbers, and if one of them is lucky, they'll send you a prize.
jokes.setup: Discussing telemarketing scams
jokes.punchline: Jacqueline completely misunderstanding how scams work, thinking she's helping telemarketers
jokes.explanation: Brilliant inversion - elderly victim explaining scam as if she's doing charity. Perfect character writing showing naive optimism

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: Those poor telemarketers, working such long hours for so little pay. I always make sure to stay on the line as long as possible so they can meet their quotas.

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: Mom, that's exactly what they want you to do.

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: Well of course, dear. And I always give them my credit card number right away so they don't have to keep asking. I'm very efficient.

jokes.text: I was just happy to talk to someone on the phone.
jokes.setup: Explaining why she falls for phone scams
jokes.punchline: Her loneliness makes her vulnerable to any human contact
jokes.explanation: Touching and sad character moment that explains her vulnerability. Balances humor with genuine pathos

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: Her loneliness makes her vulnerable to any human contact

jokes.text: And here's a picture of me getting arrested for indecent exposure. It was the most embarrassing day of my life.
jokes.setup: Showing old photos to Grampa
jokes.punchline: Casually showing arrest photo as if it's a normal memory
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor - inappropriate oversharing treated as normal reminiscence. Reveals wild past

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: Oh, and here's one from my wild days.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: My word! Is that a mugshot?

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: Yes, but I was acquitted... mostly.

jokes.text: Oh, who were your friends? / Oh, Zelda Fitzgerald, Frances Farmer and little Sylvia Plath.
jokes.setup: Asking about her friends from her wild youth
jokes.punchline: All famous women known for tragic mental health issues and early deaths
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor - all three friends were real people known for tragic ends. Shows deep cultural knowledge and dark wit

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: So who were your friends back in your wild youth?

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: Oh, I ran around with Sylvia Plath, Marilyn Monroe, and Judy Garland.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Wow, famous women known for tragic mental health issues and early deaths!

jokes.text: You remind me of a poem I can't remember, and a song that may never have existed, and a place I'm not sure I've ever been to.
jokes.setup: Grampa trying to be romantic
jokes.punchline: Poetry that becomes increasingly uncertain and nonsensical
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating structure and perfect Grampa characterization - romantic but addled. Each clause gets more uncertain. Highly quotable and original

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Roses are red, violets are blue, I'm thinking of something, and it involves you.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Your eyes are like... uh... shiny things. And your hair smells like... hair products?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I love you more than I love... waffles? No wait, I definitely love waffles more. But you're a close second!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Let's grow old together, assuming I'm not already dead. Am I moving? I can't feel my legs.

jokes.text: I'm in love! No, wait, it's a stroke. No, wait, it is love!
jokes.setup: Grampa feeling funny after his romantic moment
jokes.punchline: Confusing love with medical emergency and back again
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure with dark humor about elderly health concerns. Perfect character moment for Grampa's age

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I feel funny... is this love?

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: Dad, you're having a heart attack!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: No, no, it's gotta be love. I'm too old to have a heart attack.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Dad, you had one last Tuesday.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: That was love too!

jokes.text: Hello, I'm Troy McClure. You might remember me from such films as The Boatjacking of Supership 79 and Hydro: The Man With the Hydraulic Arms.
jokes.setup: Troy McClure's standard introduction format
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous fake movie titles that sound like B-grade action films
jokes.explanation: Classic Troy McClure format with great fake movie titles. Perfect character integration and good satire of B-movie culture

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such films as 'Pukahontas' and 'The Eraser: Part II'.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: But today I'm here to talk about my new picture: 'The President's Neck is Missing'.

jokes.text: Yeah, 'groovy.'
jokes.setup: Referring to Generation X viewers
jokes.punchline: Troy's sarcastic, out-of-touch attempt at youth slang
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Troy's disconnection from youth culture. Simple but effective

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such educational videos as 'Reaching Out to Generation X.' Well, I've been reading about you kids and your 'radical' attitudes. So I picked up a skateboard, some baggy pants, and I'm ready to get jiggy with it!

jokes.text: Uh, do you accept the 'Federal Breast Inspector's' card? 'License to Ogle'? 'Vyza'?
jokes.setup: Homer trying to order with credit card over phone
jokes.punchline: Series of fake, inappropriate novelty 'credit cards'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of increasingly inappropriate fake cards. Shows Homer's juvenile humor and financial irresponsibility

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'd like to order some stuff. My credit card number is... uh... let me find it.

dialogue.speaker: Operator
dialogue.line: Sir, that's not a valid credit card number.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, how about this one: 'The Squishy Master 3000.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Or my 'All You Can Eat Pass' from the Frying Dutchman.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ooh, ooh! I got a Blockbuster Video card!

dialogue.speaker: Operator
dialogue.line: Sir, please—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: How about my 'Frequent Buyer' card from Krusty Burger? I only need one more donut to get a free... wait, that's at Lard Lad.

jokes.text: Oh, yes, of course. That's what I meant, 'Visa.' It's these new dentures.
jokes.setup: Homer saying 'Vyza' instead of 'Visa'
jokes.punchline: Blaming mispronunciation on dentures he doesn't have
jokes.explanation: Homer's typical excuse-making and lying. Functional character comedy but not exceptional

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's Vyza, not Visa.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: These dang dentures keep me from pronouncing it right.

dialogue.speaker: Other Character
dialogue.line: Homer, you don't have dentures.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's what makes it even worse!

jokes.text: Well, I'm opposed to the whole thing-- damn opposed! / He's damn opposed. Damn, damn, damn opposed.
jokes.setup: Homer expresses strong opposition
jokes.punchline: Bart enthusiastically amplifies the profanity
jokes.explanation: Bart's gleeful participation in swearing escalation - classic kid behavior

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, and eat my shorts!

jokes.text: If he marries your mother, Marge, we'll be brother and sister! And then our kids, they'll be horrible freaks with pink skin, no overbites and five fingers on each hand!
jokes.setup: Homer opposing Grampa marrying Jacqueline
jokes.punchline: Describing normal human features as horrific mutations
jokes.explanation: Brilliant inversion - normal features described as freakish from Simpson perspective. Great meta-humor about the show's art style

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad, you can't marry her. Look at her! She's got two eyes, a nose, and a mouth. It's like something out of a nightmare!

jokes.text: Tell her I love her! / Grampa, shh! I know my mother. If you come on too strong, she's going to get scared away. / I love ya. / What? / Uh, I love ya, Mom.
jokes.setup: Grampa wanting to express love through Marge on the phone
jokes.punchline: Awkward confusion where Marge accidentally says 'I love ya' to her mother
jokes.explanation: Good escalating confusion with phone miscommunication. Creates appropriate awkwardness

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Tell your mother I love her.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Dad, why don't you tell her yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I'm too shy. You tell her.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Okay, okay. Mom? Dad says he loves ya.

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: Well, tell him I love ya too.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: She says she loves ya too, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well tell her I love her more.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I love ya more, Mom!

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: I love you more, Marge!

jokes.text: I love ya. / What? / Uh, I love ya, Mom. / I love ya. I love ya. / I love ya. I love ya. / I love ya, Mom. Gotta go.
jokes.setup: Grampa accidentally says 'I love ya' to Jacqueline
jokes.punchline: Awkward multiplication of love declarations to cover it up
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of awkwardness as everyone tries to cover the mistake

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I love ya!

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I love ya... times three! And Marge, I love ya times two!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Dad, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: It's math, woman! Nothing weird about it!

jokes.text: Oh! That is so cool! That is so exciting! That is so-- / Crappy.
jokes.setup: Lisa's enthusiasm building about the animation cel
jokes.punchline: Abrupt deflation when she sees how worthless it is
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection structure with Lisa's enthusiasm building then crashing. Well-timed deflation

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This animation cel is absolutely pristine! Do you know what this is worth?

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: Worst. Investment. Ever.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: What? But it's a genuine piece of animation history!

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: It's worth approximately... three dollars.

jokes.setup: Lisa asking how much the worthless cel cost
jokes.punchline: Revealing the absurdly high price for junk
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - just the number delivers the punchline. Shows Homer's gullibility and financial irresponsibility

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: How much did you pay for that worthless cel?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Three thousand dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: THREE THOUSAND DOLLARS?!

jokes.text: Hot diggity! I'm gonna smooch her like a mule eating an apple!
jokes.setup: Grampa excited about his romantic plans
jokes.punchline: Bizarre simile comparing kissing to animal feeding
jokes.explanation: Hilarious and original simile that perfectly captures Grampa's old-fashioned rural expressions. Highly quotable and character-specific

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I'm gonna kiss her so hard, it'll be like a horse eatin' a bucket of oats!

jokes.text: Now what's that rule? / Play it cool.
jokes.setup: Homer teaching Grampa dating advice in song
jokes.punchline: Grampa learning the lesson in the musical format
jokes.explanation: Functional conclusion to the musical bit but not particularly strong. Basic callback structure

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: ♪ When you see a lady that you like, don't be shy ♪

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: ♪ Walk right up and look her in the eye ♪

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: ♪ Compliment her shoes, or her hat ♪

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: ♪ Say something nice, leave it at that ♪

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: ♪ Oh, I never knew that's what I should do ♪

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: ♪ I was too busy being me, not being true ♪

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: ♪ But now I understand, thanks to my son ♪

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: ♪ I'm gonna try these moves on every single one ♪

jokes.text: This-- This is an arm drawn by nobody. It is worth nothing.
jokes.setup: Explaining the difference between valuable and worthless animation cels
jokes.punchline: Brutal honest assessment of Homer's purchase
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy delivery - blunt and condescending. Good economy in the dismissal

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: Well, you see, a valuable animation cel is one that was used in the show, hand-painted, and signed by the animator. A worthless one is... well, that one you bought.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But the guy said it was from the first episode!

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: Yes, well, he also said it would increase in value. Worst. Purchase. Ever.

jokes.text: Can't you give me anything for it? / I can give you this telephone. It is shaped like Mary Worth.
jokes.setup: Bart asking for any value for the worthless cel
jokes.punchline: Offered a novelty phone shaped like an obscure comic character
jokes.explanation: Good absurd trade and obscure cultural reference. Mary Worth is a perfect choice for a bizarre novelty phone

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Is there any value to this worthless cel?

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: Well, I could offer you this novelty phone shaped like an obscure comic character.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's it?

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: It's shaped like Radioactive Man's lesser-known sidekick, Fallout Boy. Collectors would kill for this.

jokes.text: Hey!
jokes.setup: Grampa being cut in on by Mr. Burns while dancing
jokes.punchline: Grampa's indignant reaction to being muscled aside
jokes.explanation: Simple reaction beat. Functional but minimal comedic value on its own

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Hey! I was dancing with her!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, well, now you're not.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Why you... I'll have you know I'm a very good dancer!

jokes.text: That big ape thinks he can muscle in on my girl, does he? I'll show him. I'm gonna give him the frowning of a lifetime.
jokes.setup: Grampa angry about Burns cutting in on his dance
jokes.punchline: Threatening someone with an aggressive frown instead of violence
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from anger to hilariously mild threat. 'Frowning of a lifetime' is perfectly Grampa-esque

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Why you...

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I'm gonna give you such a pinch!

jokes.text: I-- Who told you? / Oh, yes, yes, well. I'd say you were an angel, but angels don't dance like that.
jokes.setup: Jacqueline calling Burns 'the devil himself'
jokes.punchline: Burns accidentally confirming it before recovering with smooth talk
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Burns accidentally revealing his evil nature then smoothly covering. Good structure with quick recovery

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: You're the devil himself!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Why yes, I— I mean, no! Certainly not.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I'm merely a humble industrialist devoted to the betterment of Springfield and the happiness of my employees.

jokes.text: Mr. Simpson, I represent the estate of Jimmy Durante. I have a court order demanding an immediate halt to this unauthorized imitation-- Boys!
jokes.setup: Grampa doing his Jimmy Durante impression
jokes.punchline: Legal action against celebrity impersonation taken to absurd extremes
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the earlier Chaplin lawsuit bit. Shows the absurd legal overreach continuing

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Cha-cha-cha-cha-cha! Ha-cha-cha!

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Cease and desist! You are in violation of the Jimmy Durante Estate's intellectual property rights.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: But I'm just doing an impression!

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: That'll be $10,000 in damages, plus court fees.

jokes.text: Well, would it be all right with you if I just laid down in the street and died? / Yes, that would be acceptable.
jokes.setup: Grampa asking sarcastically if he can die in the street
jokes.punchline: Lawyer matter-of-factly agreeing that would be fine
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with perfect deadpan response. The lawyer's matter-of-fact acceptance of death is perfectly cold

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: So I can just die in the street?

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Oh yes, that would be fine.

jokes.text: 130-- But only one since I've become a billionaire.
jokes.setup: Burns asking how many women have said no to him
jokes.punchline: Revealing his wealth usually ensures compliance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns characterization showing how wealth corrupts relationships. Dark implication delivered matter-of-factly

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: How many women have said no to me?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Well, sir, I'd have to say... all of them.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Ah, but I always get my way in the end. You see, I simply reveal my wealth, and they come crawling back.

jokes.text: Whoop-dee-do, sir. / Yes, whoop-dee-do. Whoop-dee-do to the world! Whoop-dee-do, Mr. Florist. Whoop-dee-do, Mr. Physical Trainer. Whoop-dee-do, Mr. President.
jokes.setup: Smithers' sarcastic response to Burns being in love
jokes.punchline: Burns taking it literally and escalating to absurd proportions
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Burns' obliviousness to sarcasm. Character-appropriate but not exceptional

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Oh yes, sir, you're absolutely head over heels in love.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes! I shall build a 90-foot statue of her in the town square!

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, I was being sarcastic.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Too late! I've already ordered the marble!

jokes.text: Whoop-dee-do to the world! Whoop-dee-do, Mr. Florist. Whoop-dee-do, Mr. Physical Trainer. Whoop-dee-do, Mr. President.
jokes.setup: Burns adopts Smithers' sarcastic phrase
jokes.punchline: Escalates to addressing increasingly important figures
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Burns' grandiosity, though somewhat repetitive

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, you're fired.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Yes, sir. Very droll.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Oh, so you think that's funny, do you? Well, I'm going to tell the Mayor you're a communist.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: And then I'll call the Governor and tell him you're a murderer.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Then I'll phone the President and tell him you're... well, I'll think of something.

jokes.text: Everyone who's found true love may leave early today. / All right!
jokes.setup: Burns announcing special privilege for those in love
jokes.punchline: Workers immediately taking advantage despite obviously not being in love
jokes.explanation: Good workplace humor showing employees gaming the system. Predictable but functional

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I'm feeling generous today. Any couple in love may leave early.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Come on, Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I love you, Lenny!

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Me too, Homer!

jokes.text: I... sent it to you on your birthday.
jokes.setup: Burns asking how Smithers wrote such a romantic note so quickly
jokes.punchline: Revealing Smithers had written it for Burns originally
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment revealing Smithers' unrequited feelings. Perfect structure with uncomfortable revelation

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Well sir, I didn't write it for you. I wrote it for myself... to you.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: What?!

jokes.text: Why it's Fred Flintstone and his lovely wife, Wilma. Oh, and this must be little Pebbles. / Yabba dabba do!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to be polite to Mr. Burns at the door
jokes.punchline: Comparing the wealthy elderly couple to cartoon cavemen
jokes.explanation: Homer's characteristic pop culture confusion. Burns playing along with catchphrase is nice touch

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns! Come in, come in! Can I get you anything? A drink? Some food?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: No thank you, Simpson. I'm just here to discuss your performance at the plant.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you know, Mr. Burns, you and Mrs. Burns are like the Flintstones!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: The Flintstones? How dare you compare me to that common cartoon rabble!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, no, it's a compliment! You're wealthy and powerful, just like Fred and Barney... except you're old and rich and they're young and poor.

jokes.text: Mind if I come in? I brought chocolates. / Yabba dabba do!
jokes.setup: Burns tries to be charming
jokes.punchline: Homer continues the Flintstones bit
jokes.explanation: Simple callback - Homer's commitment to the bit despite its inappropriateness

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Ah, Simpson. You know, I've always fancied myself a man of the people. Why, just this morning I was watching that delightful cartoon... what is it called? The Flintstones?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yabba-dabba-doo!

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, quite amusing. Say, wouldn't you like to join me for a spot of—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wilma! Get me another bronto-burger!

jokes.text: That's right, 'Money.' Your 'money's' happiness is all that 'moneys.'
jokes.setup: Homer trying to be supportive about his mother's happiness
jokes.punchline: Getting confused and replacing words with 'money' incorrectly
jokes.explanation: Homer's linguistic confusion when trying to think about money. Character-appropriate but not highly memorable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mom, I'm really happy you're happy. That's what matters.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's sweet, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, because money is the root of all... happiness. Wait, no. Happiness is the root of all money? Ugh, I'm just gonna say it: you make me money, Mom.

jokes.text: Hi. You know, Grandma really likes it when her boyfriends are nice to me. She especially likes it when they give me money.
jokes.setup: Bart approaches Burns with innocent observation
jokes.punchline: Reveals his manipulative scheme
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart manipulation - precocious scheming delivered with false innocence

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, I've been thinking about your safety. You know, for someone your age, you're in pretty good shape.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Why thank you, boy. That's very kind of you to say.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I was thinking maybe I could be your personal safety consultant. You know, make sure nothing bad happens to you.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Excellent idea! You're hired!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Cool. My first recommendation is that you change your will to make me your sole heir.

jokes.text: Run along, peewee. Baby gets nothing. / Very well, you leave me no choice. This gun is filled with ketchup. This one with mustard. Now give me $350 bucks or you're gonna be a mess for your date.
jokes.setup: Burns dismisses Bart
jokes.punchline: Bart produces condiment weapons with specific monetary demand
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - Bart's elaborate revenge plan with hilariously non-lethal weapons

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Get out of my office, boy.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well, I've got ketchup, mustard, and relish. That'll be fifty bucks.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Condiments? You're holding me up with condiments?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: You heard me, old man. Fifty bucks or you're gonna look like a hot dog.

jokes.text: Run along, peewee. Baby gets nothing. / Very well, you leave me no choice. This gun is filled with ketchup. This one with mustard. Now give me $350 bucks or you're gonna be a mess for your date.
jokes.setup: Burns dismissing Bart's request for money
jokes.punchline: Bart threatening Burns with condiment guns for the exact amount Homer spent
jokes.explanation: Great escalation and callback to the $350. Bart's creative threat method is character-perfect and surprisingly effective

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Give me money or I'll shoot you with my condiment gun!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: A condiment gun? That's the most ridiculous thing I've ever heard. Get out!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Fine! But I'm taking exactly $5,000 - the amount my dad blew on donuts last month!

jokes.text: You don't have the guts.
jokes.setup: Bart threatening Burns with condiment guns
jokes.punchline: Burns calling his bluff, treating it like a real threat
jokes.explanation: Burns treating ketchup guns like lethal weapons. Good setup for visual gag that presumably follows

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Back off, Burns! I've got condiment guns!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Condiment guns? You think you can threaten me with... *adjusts monocle* ...mustard and relish? I've faced down union organizers, government regulators, and my own mortality. Release the hounds!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa! I'm just messing with you!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Too late, Simpson. Guards, call in the National Guard. We're treating this as a Level 5 condiment emergency.

jokes.text: Here. Take your money. Take it. / Now, the tables have turned, haven't they?
jokes.setup: Burns pays up
jokes.punchline: Bart's dramatic villain monologue
jokes.explanation: Bart's moment of triumph with appropriately dramatic delivery

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: You know, Mr. Burns, you're not a bad guy. You're just a guy who does bad things. But now you've paid up, and that makes you... well, still pretty bad, actually. But at least you're bad with money now instead of just bad with people. Wait, no, you're still bad with people. Aw man, I'm not good at villain monologues.

jokes.text: Uh-oh. Better call the nurse.
jokes.setup: Presumably someone is having medical distress
jokes.punchline: Casual reaction to emergency
jokes.explanation: Standard nursing home emergency gag - predictable

jokes.text: Whoo-hoo! $350. Now, I can buy 70 transcripts of Nightline.
jokes.setup: Homer happy to get the $350 back from Bart
jokes.punchline: Planning to spend it on something equally useless
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - avoiding one bad purchase only to plan another equally pointless one. Nightline transcripts is absurdly specific

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm going to spend it on something equally useless.

jokes.text: Hey, Bart. You wanna go play with that X-ray machine in the abandoned hospital?
jokes.setup: Phone call asking Bart to play
jokes.punchline: Suggesting extremely dangerous activity with medical equipment
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about childhood recklessness taken to dangerous extremes. Efficient setup of hazardous play

dialogue.speaker: Phone caller
dialogue.line: Hey Bart, want to come over and play?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Sure, what do you want to do?

dialogue.speaker: Phone caller
dialogue.line: Well, my dad just brought home a defibrillator from the hospital. We could use it to shock each other!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, I think I'll pass on that one.

jokes.text: Oh! Mom, you can't marry Mr. Burns. He's an evil man. / Evil, schmevil, Marge. Monty can provide for me.
jokes.setup: Marge pointing out Burns is evil
jokes.punchline: Jacqueline dismissing evil as unimportant compared to wealth
jokes.explanation: 'Evil, schmevil' is perfect dismissive language. Shows priorities clearly with good economy

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But Mr. Burns is evil!

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: Evil, Marge? Who cares about evil when he's so rich?

jokes.text: But compared to Mr. Burns, he's Judge freaking Reinhold.
jokes.setup: Defending Grampa against Burns
jokes.punchline: Using obscure 80s actor as pinnacle of virtue
jokes.explanation: Brilliant random celebrity reference. Judge Reinhold as ultimate good guy is perfectly absurd and specific

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, Grampa Simpson is a good man. He's honest, hardworking, and decent.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Decent? Why, he's no better than that fellow... what's his name... David Caruso.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: David Caruso? He's a wonderful actor!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Exactly my point.

jokes.text: 'La-tex Con-dom.' Boy, I'd like to live in one of those!
jokes.setup: Grampa reading something he found on the ground
jokes.punchline: Completely misunderstanding what a condom is
jokes.explanation: Perfect innocent misunderstanding from elderly character. Good callback to his earlier statement about ground reading

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, what do we have here? A rubber... something. Must be a new kind of balloon!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I'll inflate it and use it to keep my dentures fresh!

jokes.text: Hey! Down in front! / Nein!
jokes.setup: Someone blocking view at wedding
jokes.punchline: German person refusing to move
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag with minimal impact. Foreign language adds slight twist but overall weak

dialogue.speaker: Wedding Guest
dialogue.line: Excuse me, you're blocking my view of the ceremony.

dialogue.speaker: German Person
dialogue.line: Nein! I vas here first!

jokes.text: Yeah, but his reply envelope just had a check to the gas company in it.
jokes.setup: Explaining why Grampa isn't at the wedding
jokes.punchline: He sent his gas bill payment instead of RSVP
jokes.explanation: Perfect elderly confusion joke. Specific detail about gas company makes it more believable and funny

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: He sent his gas bill payment instead of RSVP

jokes.text: We've heard enough about Bliz-blaz and Him-ham already. Get to the bloody point!
jokes.setup: Long biblical wedding ceremony reading
jokes.punchline: Burns mangling biblical names and demanding speed
jokes.explanation: Burns' impatience and butchering of biblical names is character-appropriate. 'Bliz-blaz and Him-ham' is nicely dismissive

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Now, where was I? Oh yes, Deuteronomy, Chapter 3, Verse... uh... 6. 'And Balaam said unto the ass...' Skip ahead! Skip ahead! This is taking forever! I don't have all day. Move it along!

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: But sir, we're only on the Old Testament...

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I said SKIP AHEAD! And what's with all these unpronounceable names? Methuselah? Jehoshaphat? This better wrap up in the next five minutes or I'm cutting the check!

jokes.text: The ring, you little imbecile, before I really lose my temper.
jokes.setup: Ring bearer hesitating
jokes.punchline: Burns threatening a child at his own wedding
jokes.explanation: Burns being cruel to children is standard character behavior. Functional but not exceptional

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Well? Don't just stand there, boy. Put the ring on my finger or I'll have you crushed into a fine powder and mixed into my cereal.

dialogue.speaker: Ring Bearer
dialogue.line: But sir, I'm only five...

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Your age is irrelevant to me. Now move, before I decide to make you into a throw pillow.

jokes.text: Honey, are you sure you wanna be Mrs. Montgomery Burns? Wouldn't you rather be Mrs. Abraham J. Simpson? / No. / I don't wanna be either.
jokes.setup: Grampa interrupting wedding with romantic plea
jokes.punchline: Jacqueline rejecting both suitors completely
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and misdirection. Expected romantic rescue becomes double rejection. Efficient structure

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Jacqueline! I came here to tell you that I love you! Run away with me!

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: Grampa, no. I'm marrying Richard.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: But Jacqueline, we had something special!

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: That was a long time ago. And frankly, I've moved on from both of you. Richard, I'm calling off this wedding.

dialogue.speaker: Richard
dialogue.line: What?!

dialogue.speaker: Jacqueline Bouvier
dialogue.line: I'm leaving. Alone.

jokes.text: Hot diggity damn! That's good enough for me!
jokes.setup: Jacqueline doesn't want to marry either man
jokes.punchline: Grampa's enthusiastic acceptance of this rejection
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa optimism - finding victory in mutual rejection

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, that's fine by me! More time for me to spend with my stories and my oatmeal!

jokes.text: Hello, Grampa, my old friend / Your busy day is at an end / Your exploits have been sad and boring / They tell a tale that's worth ignoring
jokes.setup: Musical number about Grampa
jokes.punchline: Song brutally dismissing his life as worthless and boring
jokes.explanation: Brutal honesty wrapped in cheerful melody. Good contrast between upbeat music and harsh lyrics

dialogue.speaker: Paul Anka
dialogue.line: You're the man who has everything except for a life, Grampa Simpson. You're a bore, a kook, a creep. Your life stinks. You should be dead.

jokes.text: Oh, it was a fine machine with a vulcanized rubber listening tube which you crammed in your ear. The tube would go in easier with some sort of lubricant like linseed oil or Dr. Shumway's--
jokes.setup: Grampa explaining his attraction to Jacqueline's voice
jokes.punchline: Getting sidetracked into unnecessary technical details about old phonographs
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa rambling with period-specific details. Character integration is excellent but runs a bit long

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: It's her voice, you see. So smooth, so sultry... reminds me of the phonographs they had back in the day. You know, those Edison cylinder phonographs with the wax cylinders, recorded at 160 RPM, with the morning glory horn speaker that could project sound up to 15 feet without any electrical amplification whatsoever. And the diaphragm! Oh, the diaphragm was made of mica or sometimes glass, vibrating against a stylus made of sapphire or steel depending on the groove width of the cylinder itself...

tmdb_overview: Grandpa Simpson falls for Marge's mother, but loses out to a competitor; Bart gets ripped off shopping at home with Homer's credit card.
insight: Vintage character comedy peaks: 71 jokes in 22 minutes sustains Season 5's sweetest sweetness.
